subtopics.title: What's in the Box
subtopics.content:
#### Player-Count Reference
- **Standard game** — 1 to 10+ players.
- **Recommended** — 2 to 4 players.

#### Core Components
- **Wooden blocks** — 54 hardwood blocks, each approximately 1.5 cm x 2.5 cm x 7.5 cm.
- **Loading tray** — 1 plastic loading sleeve used to build the initial tower.
- **Rulebook** — 1 rulebook.

#### Block Specifications
- **Uniform size** — All blocks are the same dimensions, though slight manufacturing variations create differences in fit and looseness.
- **Natural wood** — Unfinished or lightly finished wood; surfaces develop different friction levels over time.

subtopics.title: Board Layout
subtopics.content:
#### Setup Sequence
1. **Use loading tray** — Stand the loading sleeve upright on a flat, stable surface.
2. **Load blocks** — Place blocks in groups of 3 side by side in the tray, alternating direction 90 degrees each level.
3. **Build 18 levels** — Stack all 54 blocks (3 per level x 18 levels).
4. **Remove tray** — Carefully slide the loading sleeve away, leaving the freestanding tower.
5. **Square the tower** — Gently adjust any misaligned blocks to make the tower as uniform as possible.

#### Tower Structure
- **Level composition** — Each level has exactly 3 blocks placed side by side.
- **Alternating direction** — Each level is perpendicular to the one below it (0/90/0/90 pattern).
- **Tower height** — 18 levels high at game start.

subtopics.title: Player Setup
subtopics.content:
#### Players
- **No roles** — All players are equal. No special roles.
- **Turn order** — Clockwise from the starting player.
- **Seating** — Players should sit or stand where they can comfortably reach the tower.

#### Playing Surface
- **Flat and stable** — Use a hard, level table. Avoid wobbly or uneven surfaces.
- **No tablecloth** — A bare table surface provides the best foundation.
- **Central placement** — Position the tower so all players have reasonable access.

subtopics.title: Starting the Game
subtopics.content:
#### Pre-First-Turn Checks
- **Tower built** — All 54 blocks are stacked in the 18-level alternating pattern.
- **Loading tray removed** — Tower is freestanding.
- **Surface stable** — Table is level and sturdy.

#### First Action
1. **Determine starting player** — The player who built the tower goes first (or any agreed method).
2. **First move** — The starting player removes one block from any level below the highest completed level and places it on top.

subtopics.title: Turn Structure
subtopics.content:
#### Turn Flow
1. **Choose a block** — Using one hand only, tap, push, or pull a block to test its looseness.
2. **Remove the block** — Carefully slide the chosen block completely out of the tower using one hand.
3. **Place on top** — Place the removed block on the topmost level of the tower, perpendicular to the level below.
4. **Complete the level** — If the top level already has 3 blocks, start a new level.
5. **Wait for stability** — Your turn does not end until the tower is stable and you have removed your hand.
6. **Next player** — Play passes to the next player clockwise.

#### Timing
- **No time limit** — There is no official time limit per turn, though house rules may add one.
- **Turn completion** — Your turn officially ends 10 seconds after you place the block on top (allowing any wobble to settle).

subtopics.title: Actions & Options
subtopics.content:
#### One-Hand Rule
- **Single hand only** — You may only use one hand at a time to remove or place blocks.
- **Hand switching** — You may switch which hand you use during your turn, but only one hand may touch the tower at any given moment.
- **No bracing** — You may not use your other hand, body, or any object to stabilize the tower.

#### Block Selection Rules
- **Below the top** — You may only remove blocks from below the highest completed level.
- **Test for looseness** — You may gently push or tap a block to see if it will slide out.
- **Switch blocks** — If a block won't come out easily, you may leave it and try a different block, provided you have not significantly disturbed the tower.
- **Return rule** — If you begin to remove a block but change your mind, you must either fully remove it or push it back to its original position using one hand.

#### Placement Rules
- **Top of tower** — Removed blocks must be placed on the topmost level.
- **Perpendicular** — The new top level must be perpendicular to the level directly below it.
- **Three per level** — Each level holds exactly 3 blocks side by side. Once 3 are placed, the level is complete.
- **Aligned** — Blocks on the same level should be parallel and adjacent.

subtopics.title: Special Mechanics
subtopics.content:
#### Tower Collapse
- **Who loses** — The player who causes the tower to fall (or any part of it to collapse) loses the game.
- **Partial collapse** — If blocks fall but the tower remains mostly standing, the group decides if the tower has "fallen." Generally, if more than one block besides the one being moved falls, the tower has collapsed.
- **Incidental contact** — If a block falls during the testing phase but the tower remains stable, the block must be placed back before continuing.

#### Looseness Variation
- **Center blocks** — Center blocks in a three-block row are often the easiest to push through.
- **Edge blocks** — Edge blocks can be tapped or slid out sideways.
- **Friction differences** — Some blocks are tighter than others due to wood variations and weight distribution.

#### Common House Rules
- **Time limit** — Set a 30-second or 60-second timer per turn to maintain pace.
- **Penalty tower** — Instead of elimination, assign penalties (e.g., take a drink) for each block dropped.
- **Two-hand rule** — Some groups allow two hands for younger players.
- **No testing** — Some groups forbid testing and require committing to the first block touched.

subtopics.title: Endgame & Scoring
subtopics.content:
#### Game End
- **Tower collapse** — The game ends immediately when the tower falls.
- **Loser identified** — The player who caused the collapse loses.
- **Everyone else wins** — All other players win (or the last player who successfully completed a turn is declared the winner).

#### Scoring Model
- **No points** — Jenga has no point system.
- **Binary outcome** — You either caused the collapse (lose) or you did not (win).
- **Multi-game tracking** — Players may track losses across multiple games for a session winner.

#### Tiebreaker
- **Not applicable** — There is no tie possible. One player always causes the collapse.

subtopics.title: Opening Priorities
subtopics.content:
#### Early Game
- **Test before committing** — Gently tap or push multiple blocks to find the loosest one.
- **Center blocks first** — In the early game, center blocks are often the easiest to remove by pushing them through from one side.
- **Create options** — Removing a center block creates two loose edge blocks for future turns.
- **Stay low** — Lower levels have more weight pressing down but blocks there may still be loose.

#### Tower Reading
- **Look for gaps** — Levels with one block already removed have two blocks that may be easier to extract.
- **Check alignment** — Slightly misaligned blocks often indicate looseness.
- **Weight distribution** — Avoid removing blocks from levels that are clearly bearing significant weight.

subtopics.title: Common Mistakes
subtopics.content:
#### Frequent Errors
- **Rushing** — Moving too quickly and knocking the tower by accident.
- **Force over finesse** — Trying to muscle out a tight block instead of finding a looser alternative.
- **Forgetting one-hand rule** — Instinctively reaching with the second hand to steady the tower.
- **Top-heavy placement** — Placing blocks unevenly on top, creating a lean that compounds over time.
- **Ignoring the lean** — Not accounting for the tower's current lean direction when choosing which block to remove.
- **Commitment to a stuck block** — Continuing to pull a block that is clearly bearing weight instead of stopping and choosing another.

subtopics.title: Key Decisions
subtopics.content:
#### Decision 1: Which Block to Remove
- **Loose block** — Always prefer the loosest available block. Test several before committing.
- **Strategic removal** — In competitive play, remove a block that makes the next player's turn harder (e.g., leaving structurally critical blocks for opponents).

#### Decision 2: Push Through or Pull Out
- **Push through** — Center blocks can be pushed from one side to the other. Often safer as the tower rocks less.
- **Pull out** — Edge blocks must be pulled outward. Requires more care to avoid tipping.

#### Decision 3: Placement Position
- **Center top** — Placing blocks in the center of the top level adds stability.
- **Edge placement** — Placing on edges may be necessary but adds to lean potential.
- **Counter the lean** — Place blocks on the side opposite the tower's lean to counterbalance.
